metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Science 9 explores cellular reproduction, atomic structure and chemical bonding, electric circuits, and the interconnected systems of the biosphere. Students deepen their scientific inquiry skills, applying more complex experimental design and data analysis. First Peoples perspectives on interconnectedness and sustainability are integrated as legitimate and valuable knowledge systems alongside Western science.
metadata.general_description: An integrated science course covering cellular reproduction, atomic structure and chemistry, electric circuits, and Earth's interconnected systems — deepening scientific inquiry, data analysis, and evidence-based reasoning.

big_ideas.elaboration: All new cells arise from existing cells through the processes of mitosis and meiosis, enabling growth, repair, and reproduction in all living things.

big_ideas.statement: The electron arrangement of atoms impacts their chemical nature.
big_ideas.elaboration: The number and arrangement of electrons in an atom's outer shell determines how it bonds with other atoms to form compounds — the foundation of all chemistry.

big_ideas.statement: Electric current is the flow of electric charge.
big_ideas.elaboration: Electricity involves the movement of electrons through circuits. Understanding voltage, current, and resistance allows us to design and use electrical systems safely and effectively.

big_ideas.statement: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected, as matter cycles and energy flows through them.
big_ideas.elaboration: Earth's systems interact continuously — the water cycle, carbon cycle, and nitrogen cycle all connect living and non-living components of Earth in a dynamic, interdependent whole.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; identify increasingly complex questions about nature; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict multiple outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can ask increasingly complex scientific questions about the natural world.
I can formulate testable hypotheses based on prior knowledge and observations.
I can predict multiple possible outcomes for an investigation and explain my reasoning.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan and select investigation methods; assess risks; select appropriate equipment; follow safety and ethical guidelines.
i_can_statements: I can design and plan a scientific investigation selecting the most appropriate method.
I can identify and manage safety and ethical risks in an investigation.
I can systematically collect accurate and reliable data using appropriate equipment and SI units.
I can assess cultural and environmental factors when planning investigations.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Apply First Peoples perspectives; seek patterns, trends, and connections; construct and analyze graphs and models; analyze cause-and-effect relationships.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples perspectives and local knowledge to enrich my scientific understanding.
I can identify patterns, trends, and connections in data sets.
I can construct and interpret graphs and models to represent data.
I can analyze cause-and-effect relationships and draw conclusions consistent with evidence.

competencies.raw: Evaluate methods; identify errors and alternative explanations; consider knowledge change over time; exercise informed skepticism; consider social, ethical, and environmental implications.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate an investigation's methods and identify specific sources of error.
I can suggest concrete improvements to increase the reliability of a study.
I can evaluate the limitations of scientific models and explain what they do and don't capture.
I can consider the social, ethical, and environmental implications of a scientific development.
I can exercise informed skepticism — asking what evidence supports a claim.

competencies.raw: Transfer learning to new situations; generate refined ideas; find solutions to local/global problems through inquiry.
i_can_statements: I can apply scientific concepts to solve a real-world problem.
I can generate creative solutions and refine them based on feedback and testing.
I can connect my learning to local and global issues and propose evidence-based responses.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; communicate scientific ideas and evidence-based arguments; express varied worldviews through place.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and explain a theoretical model to describe a phenomenon.
I can communicate scientific ideas clearly using appropriate vocabulary and conventions.
I can express how local environments and diverse worldviews — including First Peoples perspectives — shape scientific understanding.

areas.name: Reproduction and Cell Division
areas.raw: Cells are derived from cells — asexual and sexual reproduction.
topics: Cell cycle: interphase, mitosis (PMAT), cytokinesis
Asexual reproduction: binary fission, budding, fragmentation, spores, vegetative reproduction, cloning, grafting
Sexual reproduction: meiosis — producing genetically unique offspring
Comparison of asexual and sexual reproduction: advantages and disadvantages
Human reproductive system: fertilization and development overview
Genetic variation and its importance for species survival

areas.name: Atomic Structure and Chemical Bonding
areas.raw: The electron arrangement of atoms impacts their chemical nature.
topics: Atomic structure: protons, neutrons, electrons; atomic number, mass number, isotopes
Electron arrangement: shells/energy levels; valence electrons
The periodic table: periods, groups, families; trends in properties
Chemical bonding: ionic bonds (metal + non-metal) and covalent bonds (non-metal + non-metal)
Lewis structures: representing valence electrons
Naming and writing formulas for ionic and covalent compounds
Polyatomic ions: hydroxide, nitrate, sulfate, carbonate, ammonium

areas.name: Electric Circuits
areas.raw: Electric current is the flow of electric charge.
topics: Electric charge: positive and negative; static electricity
Electric current: definition, flow of electrons (DC), direction
Alternating current (AC) vs. direct current (DC)
Circuit components: battery, switch, resistor, bulb, ammeter, voltmeter
Series circuits: same current throughout, voltages add
Parallel circuits: voltage same throughout, currents add
Voltage, current, and resistance: Ohm's Law (V = IR)
Safety: dangers of electricity and fuses/circuit breakers
Energy and power in circuits: P = IV

areas.name: Earth's Systems and Matter Cycles
areas.raw: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected; matter cycles and energy flows.
topics: Earth's four major systems: biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere
The water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff
The carbon cycle: photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, fossil fuels, ocean absorption
The nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, denitrification
The phosphorus cycle: weathering, uptake, decomposition
Human impacts: climate change, pollution, bioaccumulation, and biomagnification
Sustainability: systems approach to managing Earth's resources
First Peoples knowledge: interconnectedness and sustainability practices
